story_title: The Klondike Gold Rush — 100,000 Stampede to the Ends of the Earth
summary: When prospector George Carmack and his companions found gold on Rabbit Creek in Yukon Territory on 16 August 1896, they ignited the last great gold rush of the 19th century: an estimated 100,000 would-be miners attempted the brutal overland journey to the Klondike, barely 30,000 arrived, and the total output peaked at C$22 million in 1900.
recommended_arc: Hook: a prospector pans a creek and pulls up a golden nugget the size of his thumb. Rising: word hits Seattle and San Francisco; 100,000 men quit their jobs to march through the ice. Climax: Chilkoot Pass, White Pass, scurvy, and frostbite — only one in three makes it. Payoff: the stampede founds Dawson City and reshapes the Pacific Northwest economy.

verified_facts.claim: George Carmack, along with his brother-in-law Skookum Jim Mason and nephew Dawson Charlie, discovered gold on Rabbit Creek (later renamed Bonanza Creek) in the Klondike region of Yukon Territory, Canada on 16 August 1896.

News of the Klondike discovery reached the United States on 17 July 1897 when the steamship Excelsior docked in San Francisco with about $500,000 worth of Klondike gold; two days later, the Portland arrived in Seattle with prospectors carrying more than a ton of gold.
Approximately 100,000 prospectors ('stampeders') set out for the Klondike between 1897 and 1899; an estimated 30,000-40,000 actually reached Dawson City in Yukon Territory.

The North-West Mounted Police required each person crossing the Chilkoot or White Pass to carry a year's worth of supplies — roughly 1,000 pounds (450 kg) — as a condition of entry into the Yukon; most stampeders made dozens of trips up the passes to relay their gear.
Dawson City grew from a small settlement to a city of approximately 30,000 people by 1898, making it briefly the largest city north of Seattle and west of Winnipeg.

Gold production in the Klondike peaked in 1900 at approximately $22 million in Canadian dollars (roughly $600 million in 2020 values); total production from 1896 to 1903 is estimated at over C$100 million.

The Klondike Gold Rush effectively ended between 1899 and 1900 when gold was discovered in Nome, Alaska, drawing many Dawson City residents westward; by 1900 the Klondike creeks were largely worked out by the individual placer miners who had rushed in.
